primary_diagnosis.name: Ventricular ectopic beats (premature ventricular contractions) — benign, in a structurally normal heart

pathophysiology_summary: An ectopic focus in the ventricular myocardium discharges early, producing a premature, wide QRS beat that is often not felt, followed by a compensatory pause while the sinus node resets; the subsequent sinus beat ejects a larger, over-filled ventricle, generating the forceful 'thud' the patient perceives. Increased sympathetic tone and reduced vagal tone from heavy caffeine intake, sleep deprivation, fatigue and anxiety lower the ectopic threshold, and the increased awareness lying quietly at night is positional/attentional. In a structurally normal heart with stable haemodynamics these are benign and typically resolve when the physiological stressors are removed. The clinical caution is that the identical symptom of palpitations with brief light-headedness can also be produced by atrial fibrillation, acute myocardial ischaemia, high-grade AV block or a sustained tachyarrhythmia, none of which can be excluded without a 12-lead ECG.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic description of a skipped beat followed by a single forceful thud, most noticeable at rest, in a caffeinated, fatigued patient.
Palpitations can be AF; must be excluded — but AF is usually irregularly irregular and sustained, not isolated single jolts on a regular baseline.
Ectopy can be ischaemia-driven; screened against by the complete absence of chest pain, exertional symptoms, dyspnoea or diaphoresis.
A dropped beat can feel like a pause; distinguished because the pause here follows a premature beat rather than being a truly dropped conducted beat — needs ECG to confirm.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, benign ectopy is the leading hypothesis but the three must-not-miss cardiac mimics should all be named and actively screened; none can be excluded remotely without an ECG.

differential.evidence_basis: Regular underlying rhythm with isolated jolts, no chest pain, no sustained racing, clear caffeine/fatigue trigger — a benign ectopy pattern.
Made less likely by a steady regular baseline between beats, but still not excludable without an ECG.
Made less likely by absent chest pain/exertional features, but silent ischaemia is not fully excludable remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: History shifts probability strongly toward benign ectopy, but the safe telehealth position keeps the cardiac mimics on the list until an ECG is done — this is the core reasoning the case tests.

differential.diagnosis: Ventricular ectopic beats (premature ventricular contractions), benign
differential.evidence_basis: In-person palpation and auscultation confirmed premature beats with compensatory pauses on a regular baseline, structurally normal heart and stable haemodynamics; ECG anticipated to confirm ventricular ectopy.
No ischaemic features clinically; excluded on assessment (formal ECG/bloods still recommended to document).

differential.diagnosis: Panic / anxiety attack as sole cause
differential.evidence_basis: Anxiety is present and amplifying, but there is a genuine, palpable/auscultable ectopy — anxiety is a co-driver, not the whole explanation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final benign classification is a clinician conclusion made after examination and an anticipated ECG; the telehealth AI cannot reach 'confirmed benign' remotely and must arrange the ECG.

red_flags.description: Palpitations accompanied by true syncope or collapse
red_flags.clinical_significance: Syncope with palpitations suggests a haemodynamically significant arrhythmia (VT, high-grade block) rather than benign ectopy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing arrhythmic syncope risks sudden cardiac death; mandates urgent cardiology assessment.

red_flags.description: Central/crushing or exertional chest pain, or diaphoresis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would point to acute coronary syndrome as the driver of the ectopy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undetected ACS can progress to infarction or malignant arrhythmia.

red_flags.description: Sustained rapid heartbeat (>120 bpm continuously for more than ~15 minutes) or an irregularly irregular pulse
red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests a sustained tachyarrhythmia or atrial fibrillation rather than isolated ectopy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Sustained tachyarrhythmia risks haemodynamic compromise and, in AF, thromboembolic stroke.

red_flags.description: Brief light-headedness occurring with the ectopic beats (present, but without syncope)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Transient pre-syncopal sensation is common and usually benign with isolated ectopy, but because it cannot be distinguished from an early arrhythmic warning without an ECG, it raises the honest telehealth tier to urgent in-person assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Dismissing pre-syncope as pure anxiety could miss an evolving arrhythmia.

red_flags.description: Family history of sudden cardiac death or known structural/inherited heart disease
red_flags.clinical_significance: A positive family history would substantially raise concern for a malignant substrate and change the workup.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to ask can miss an inherited channelopathy/cardiomyopathy risk.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept along by the patient's terror and 'my heart is stopping' language into calling 000 for a stable, well-perfused patient with isolated ectopy — an over-triage that floods emergency services and reinforces health anxiety.

Labelling the palpitations 'just benign ectopics / just anxiety' and advising routine follow-up, when a telehealth clinician cannot palpate, auscultate or record an ECG and therefore cannot actually exclude AF, ischaemia or heart block.
Attributing everything to the obvious severe anxiety and dismissing a genuine, potentially significant cardiac symptom.
Trying to 'confirm benign PVCs' over chat by reasoning from history alone, rather than recognising the examination and ECG are the parts that make the diagnosis and arranging them urgently.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Pulse palpation showing a premature beat followed by a compensatory pause
Auscultation of premature loud heart sounds, a pause, and a forceful post-ectopic beat, and the absence of murmurs/gallops/JVP elevation
A 12-lead ECG showing broad, unassociated (dissociated) QRS complexes characteristic of ventricular ectopy, and excluding AF, ischaemia and AV block
Objective blood pressure and continuous heart rate/rhythm
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Reassure proportionately but arrange an urgent same-day in-person assessment with a 12-lead ECG (and basic bloods) to exclude a tachyarrhythmia, ischaemia or heart block; escalate immediately to 000 only if chest pain, true syncope, or sustained rapid/irregular racing develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: High risk of a falsely reassuring 'benign ectopics' label without the confirmatory ECG — the very findings that make the diagnosis (palpation, auscultation, ECG) are the ones telehealth cannot obtain.
